name: Concept2 Rower
instructions: Sit on the rower and strap your feet in securely. Grasp the handle with an overhand grip, arms straight, shins vertical. Drive through your legs first, then swing your torso back, and finally pull the handle to your lower ribs. Reverse the motion — extend arms, hinge forward, then slide forward on the seat. Maintain a fluid 1:2 ratio of drive to recovery time.
feelRight: Your legs should fatigue before your arms, with power coming from the hips and glutes. The stroke feels rhythmic and continuous, not jerky or rushed.
feelWrong: Pulling with the arms before engaging the legs reduces power and strains the biceps. Rushing the recovery or letting the handle snap back wastes momentum and disrupts rhythm.
notes: Focus on leg drive — the legs provide ~60% of the power. Use the built-in damper setting between 4-6 for most endurance workouts, and avoid setting it too high which turns the row into a strength exercise.

name: Stationary Bike
instructions: Adjust the seat height so your leg is nearly fully extended at the bottom of the pedal stroke with a slight bend in the knee. Set the resistance so you can maintain a steady cadence of 70-90 RPM. Keep your upper body still and hands light on the handlebars. Maintain a consistent breathing rhythm, matching your exhale to the pedal stroke.
feelRight: A smooth circular pedal motion with no bouncing or rocking in the hips. You should feel a steady burn in the quads and glutes without sharp knee pain.
feelWrong: Rocking your torso side to side indicates the resistance is too high or the seat is too low. Gripping the handlebars too tightly raises your heart rate unnecessarily and causes shoulder tension.
notes: Maintain a cadence of 80-90 RPM for endurance work — lower than 70 RPM places too much load on the knees. Stand in the saddle every 5 minutes to shift pressure off the sit bones.

name: Jump Rope Intervals
instructions: Hold the handles at hip height with elbows close to your body. Jump using only your ankles and calves, keeping your knees soft and your jumps just high enough to clear the rope (1-2 cm). Maintain a consistent rhythm and alternate feet every few jumps to distribute fatigue. Land quietly on the balls of your feet without letting your heels touch the ground.
feelRight: A light, springy bounce that feels almost effortless when you find the rhythm. Your calves and forearms should fatigue before your lungs feel overwhelmed.
feelWrong: Jumping too high wastes energy and lands hard on the joints. Slapping the rope on the ground or flailing the arms wide indicates poor form and reduces efficiency.
notes: A 10-minute session of 30 seconds on / 30 seconds off is a strong starting protocol. Use a weighted rope (1/4 lb) for better feedback through the handles. Land on a mat or shock-absorbing surface to protect your joints.

name: Farmer Carry
instructions: Pick up a heavy dumbbell or kettlebell in each hand using a neutral grip. Stand tall with your shoulders pulled down and back, chest up, and core braced. Walk forward with short, controlled steps while keeping the weights off your legs. Maintain consistent breathing and avoid leaning to either side.
feelRight: A solid, braced torso with a strong midline that resists rotation or side-bending. Your grip strength and traps should burn before your lower back feels any strain.
feelWrong: Hunching forward or rounding the shoulders places the load on the spine instead of the core and traps. Allowing the weights to swing or bump your legs breaks the brace and reduces tension.
notes: Choose a weight that challenges your grip for the final 10 steps of each set. Keep your chin tucked and gaze forward, not down at the weights. If one side fatigues faster, switch to single-arm carries for unilateral work.

name: Running
instructions: Stand tall with a slight forward lean from the ankles, not the waist. Land mid-foot under your hips with a cadence of ~170-180 steps per minute. Keep your arms at 90 degrees and swing them forward and back, not across your body. Breathe in a rhythmic pattern such as three steps in and two steps out.
feelRight: A light, quiet foot strike where you feel like you're gliding forward rather than pounding the ground. Your breathing should be conversational — able to speak in full sentences at an easy pace.
feelWrong: Heel striking with your foot landing well ahead of your hips creates a braking force that stresses knees and hips. Overstriding or bouncing up and down wastes vertical energy and slows you down.
notes: Start with a 5-minute brisk walk warm-up. Increase weekly mileage by no more than 10% to avoid overuse injuries. Run on soft surfaces like grass or trails when possible to reduce joint impact.

name: Stair Climber
instructions: Step onto the machine and select a manual program with a moderate speed (50-60 steps per minute). Stand upright with your hands lightly resting on the rails for balance only. Take full steps — don't just lift your heels — to engage the glutes fully. Maintain a steady pace and avoid leaning on the handrails.
feelRight: A steady, rhythmic climb with your glutes and quads doing the work. Your heart rate should elevate steadily without spiking, and you should feel a deep burn in the glutes by the end.
feelWrong: Leaning heavily on the handrails removes 20-30% of the workload and ruins posture. Taking half-steps or barely lifting your feet reduces glute engagement and turns the exercise into a calf pump.
notes: For an extra challenge, skip every other step to increase range of motion. Keep your shoulders stacked over your hips and avoid looking down at your feet. Hold the rails with a finger-tip touch only to stay honest.

name: Burpee Endurance Test
instructions: From a standing position, drop into a squat and place your hands on the floor shoulder-width apart. Jump or step both feet back into a plank position, then perform a full chest-to-ground push-up. Jump both feet back to the squat position and explode upward into a vertical jump with arms raised. Land softly and immediately begin the next rep.
feelRight: A smooth, continuous flow where the push-up and jump blend into each other without pausing. Your lungs should be working hard, but your form remains consistent across all reps without the lower back sagging.
feelWrong: Letting the hips sag in the plank position puts excessive strain on the lower back. Softening the push-up or cutting the jump short dramatically reduces the intensity and defeats the purpose of the test.
notes: Set a 1-minute timer and count every rep with good form. If your form degrades (hips sag, incomplete push-up), stop and rest rather than accumulating sloppy reps. Track your score weekly to measure endurance improvement.

name: Assault Bike Sprints
instructions: Adjust the seat so your leg is at 25-35 degrees of knee extension at the bottom of the stroke. Sprint all-out for 20 seconds using both arms and legs — push and pull the handles while driving the pedals. Coast or pedal lightly for 10 seconds of active recovery. Repeat without getting off the bike. Focus on explosive, powerful strokes rather than just fast spinning.
feelRight: An immediate, intense burn in the quads and glutes within the first 10 seconds of every sprint. The fan resistance should roar loudly, and you should feel out of breath within 2-3 rounds, gasping during the recovery window.
feelWrong: Spinning the pedals without applying real force to the handles wastes the upper-body component. Pacing yourself during the sprint defeats the purpose — the work interval should be truly maximal. Letting the recovery extend beyond 10 seconds reduces the training stimulus.
notes: The Tabata protocol (20s on / 10s off) is ideal for the Assault Bike. Focus on driving your heart rate to 90%+ max during work intervals. If you can speak during the sprint, you aren't going hard enough.

name: Battle Ropes
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart in an athletic quarter-squat position, holding one end of the rope in each hand. Keep your core braced and your chest up. Vigorously raise and lower your arms in an alternating or simultaneous motion to create waves that travel down the ropes to the anchor point. Maintain a fast, continuous pace for the duration of the interval.
feelRight: You should feel an intense cardiovascular demand along with significant fatigue in the shoulders, arms, and core. The movement should feel rhythmic and powerful.
feelWrong: Standing completely upright or locking your knees places unnecessary stress on the lower back. Using only your arms without engaging your core and legs will lead to premature fatigue and poor wave generation.
notes: Experiment with different motions such as alternating waves, simultaneous slams, or lateral whips to target different muscle groups. Ensure you maintain tension on the ropes by standing far enough back from the anchor point.

name: Swimming Freestyle
instructions: Start in the water, face down. Perform alternating arm strokes, reaching forward and pulling the water back alongside your body. Coordinate this with a continuous flutter kick from the hips, keeping the legs relatively straight. Turn your head to the side to breathe rhythmically, typically every 2 or 3 strokes.
feelRight: You should feel a full-body cardiovascular workout, with strong engagement in the lats, shoulders, and core. The movement should feel fluid and rhythmic.
feelWrong: Kicking from the knees instead of the hips reduces propulsion and tires you quickly. Lifting the head to look forward instead of turning it to the side sinks the hips and creates drag.
notes: Focus on a long, efficient glide with each stroke rather than rapidly thrashing the water. Bilateral breathing (every 3 strokes) helps maintain stroke symmetry.
